hints: Frequency adverbs (always, never, usually) go before the main verb in Present Simple.
The verb `goes` is the main verb — `always` must come directly before it.

a1.always: Correct: `always` goes before the main verb `goes`.
a1.goes always: Incorrect: frequency adverbs do not go after the main verb in Present Simple.
a1.always goes to: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb `always`, not extra words.

hints: After the verb `be` (am/is/are), frequency adverbs come after it.
The structure is `I am ___` — `never` goes after `am`.

a2.never: Correct: after `be`, frequency adverbs follow: `I am never late`.
a2.late never: Incorrect: frequency adverbs do not go after the adjective.
a2.not never: Incorrect: `not never` is a double negative, which is non-standard in English.

hints: Manner adverbs go after the verb or after the object.
The verb `plays` has the object `football`, so the manner adverb `well` goes after the object.

a3.well: Correct: `well` is a manner adverb placed after the object `football`.
a3.good: Incorrect: `good` is an adjective, not an adverb. Use `well` to describe how someone plays.
a3.well plays: Incorrect: manner adverbs typically do not go before the verb.

hints: Place adverbs (here, there, outside) go after the verb.
The verb `live` has no object, so the place adverb `here` goes right after it.

a4.here: Correct: `here` is a place adverb after the verb `live`.
a4.here live: Incorrect: the place adverb should follow the verb, not precede it.
a4.live here they: Incorrect: the subject cannot go after the adverb in a standard declarative sentence.

hints: Time adverbials usually go at the end of the sentence.
After the verb `eat` and the object `breakfast`, the time adverbial `every morning` takes the final position.

a5.every morning: Correct: the time adverbial `every morning` goes at the end of the sentence.
a5.every morning eat: Incorrect: time adverbials do not split the verb and object this way.
a5.morning every: Incorrect: the word order in the time phrase is fixed: `every morning`.

hints: Degree adverbs (very, too, really) go before the adjective they modify.
The adjective `small` needs the degree adverb before it: `very small`.

a6.small very: Incorrect: degree adverbs do not go after the adjective.
a6.much: Incorrect: `much` is not used before a simple adjective. Use `very` before `small`.

hints: Frequency adverbs go before the main verb.
The main verb is `eats` — `sometimes` must come before it.

a7.sometimes: Correct: `sometimes` goes before the main verb `eats`.
a7.eats sometimes: Incorrect: in this gap position, the adverb must precede the verb.
a7.sometime: Incorrect: `sometime` (without -s) means 'at some point in the future', not 'occasionally'.

hints: After `be`, frequency adverbs go after the verb.
In the structure `He is ___ happy`, `always` goes after `is`.

a8.always: Correct: after `is`, the frequency adverb goes before the adjective: `is always happy`.
a8.happy always: Incorrect: the frequency adverb does not go after the adjective.
a8.always is: Incorrect: the frequency adverb goes after `be`, not before it in a positive sentence.

hints: Frequency adverbs go before the main verb in a declarative sentence.
The main verb is `watch` — `usually` goes right before it.

a9.usually: Correct: `usually` goes before the main verb `watch`.
a9.watch usually: Incorrect: frequency adverbs do not go between the main verb and its object.
a9.usual: Incorrect: `usual` is an adjective. The adverb form is `usually`.

hints: Manner adverbs go after the verb and its object.
After `speaks English`, the manner adverb `slowly` takes the final position.

a10.slowly: Correct: `slowly` is a manner adverb after the object `English`.
a10.slow: Incorrect: `slow` is an adjective. Use the adverb `slowly` to describe the action.
a10.slowly speaks: Incorrect: manner adverbs typically do not precede the verb.

hints: Degree adverbs go before the adjective.
In the structure `is ___ tired`, the degree adverb `really` goes before `tired`.

a11.really: Correct: `really` goes before the adjective `tired` as a degree adverb.
a11.tired really: Incorrect: degree adverbs do not go after the adjective.
a11.real: Incorrect: `real` is an adjective. Use the adverb `really`.

hints: Place adverbs go after the verb.
The verb `play` has no object, so `outside` goes right after it.

a12.outside: Correct: `outside` is a place adverb after the verb `play`.
a12.outside play: Incorrect: place adverbs go after the verb, not before it.
a12.the outside: Incorrect: `the outside` is a noun phrase; the adverb `outside` is needed here.

hints: Time adverbs go at the end of the sentence.
After `go to bed`, the time adverb `early` takes the final position.

a13.early: Correct: `early` is a time adverb at the end of the sentence.
a13.early go: Incorrect: `early` does not go before the verb in this sentence type.
a13.earlily: Incorrect: the form `earlily` does not exist. `Early` is both an adjective and adverb.

hints: Frequency adverbs go before the main verb.
Before `drinks`, the frequency adverb `often` indicates regularity.

a14.often: Correct: `often` goes before the main verb `drinks`.
a14.drinks often: Incorrect: in this gap, the adverb must go before the verb.
a14.oftenly: Incorrect: the form `oftenly` does not exist. `Often` is already an adverb.

hints: Manner adverbs go after the verb when there is no object.
The verb `sings` has no object, so `beautifully` goes right after it.

a15.beautifully: Correct: `beautifully` is a manner adverb after the verb `sings`.
a15.beautiful: Incorrect: `beautiful` is an adjective. Use the adverb `beautifully`.
a15.beautifully sings: Incorrect: manner adverbs typically go after the verb.

hints: The degree adverb `too` goes before the adjective and means 'excessively'.
In `is ___ hot`, `too` goes before `hot`.

a16.too: Correct: `too` goes before the adjective `hot`, meaning 'excessively hot'.
a16.hot too: Incorrect: `too` meaning 'excessively' does not go after the adjective.
a16.to: Incorrect: `to` is a preposition, not a degree adverb. The adverb is `too` (with double 'o').

hints: Frequency adverbs go before the main verb.
Before `forget`, the frequency adverb `sometimes` is needed.

a17.sometimes: Correct: `sometimes` goes before the main verb `forget`.
a17.forget sometimes: Incorrect: in this gap, the adverb must precede the verb.
a17.some times: Incorrect: `some times` (two words) means 'a few occasions', not 'occasionally'. Use `sometimes`.

hints: Time adverbials usually go at the end of the sentence.
After `went to the park`, the time adverbial `yesterday` takes the final position.

a18.yesterday: Correct: `yesterday` is a time adverbial at the end of the sentence.
a18.yesterday went: Incorrect: in a declarative sentence, `yesterday` typically goes at the end.
a18.the yesterday: Incorrect: `yesterday` is not used with the article `the`.

hints: Adverbs go between a modal verb and the main verb.
In `can ___ speak`, `already` goes between `can` and `speak`.

a19.already: Correct: `already` goes between the modal `can` and the main verb `speak`.
a19.can already speak: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb `already`, not surrounding words.
a19.already can: Incorrect: `already` does not go before the modal `can` in a positive sentence.

hints: Manner adverbs go after the verb or between the verb and a prepositional phrase.
After `walked`, `quickly` describes the manner of walking.

a20.quickly: Correct: `quickly` is a manner adverb after the verb `walked`.
a20.quick: Incorrect: `quick` is an adjective. Use the adverb `quickly`.
a20.quickly walked: Incorrect: in this gap, the adverb goes after the verb.

hints: In the Perfect tenses, adverbs go between the auxiliary `has/have` and the past participle.
In `has ___ been`, `never` goes between `has` and `been`.

a21.never: Correct: `never` goes between `has` and `been` in the Present Perfect.
a21.been never: Incorrect: the adverb does not go after the past participle `been`.
a21.never has: Incorrect: `never has` at the beginning requires subject-verb inversion, which is not needed here.

hints: When multiple adverbials are present, follow the order: Manner–Place–Time.
After `played`, the manner adverb `happily` goes first, then place `in the garden`, then time `yesterday`.

a22.happily: Correct: `happily` is a manner adverb that goes first after the verb in the MPT order.
a22.happy: Incorrect: `happy` is an adjective. Use the adverb `happily`.
a22.happily played: Incorrect: the manner adverb goes after the verb, not before it.

hints: Frequency adverbs go before the main verb.
Before `finishes`, `usually` indicates regularity.

a23.usually: Correct: `usually` goes before the main verb `finishes`.
a23.finishes usually: Incorrect: frequency adverbs do not go after the verb in mid-position.
a23.usual: Incorrect: `usual` is an adjective. Use the adverb `usually`.

hints: Degree adverbs go before the adjective.
In `was ___ interesting`, `quite` goes before `interesting`.

a24.interesting quite: Incorrect: degree adverbs do not go after the adjective.
a24.quit: Incorrect: `quit` is a verb meaning 'to stop'. The degree adverb is `quite`.

hints: Adverbs go between the auxiliary and the main verb.
In `will ___ call`, `definitely` goes between `will` and `call`.

a25.definitely: Correct: `definitely` goes between the auxiliary `will` and the main verb `call`.
a25.will definitely call: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb, not the surrounding words.
a25.definite: Incorrect: `definite` is an adjective. Use the adverb `definitely`.

hints: After `be`, frequency adverbs go after the verb.
In `are ___ late`, `often` goes between `are` and `late`.

a26.often: Correct: after `are`, the frequency adverb goes before the adjective: `are often late`.
a26.late often: Incorrect: frequency adverbs do not go after the adjective.
a26.oftenly: Incorrect: the form `oftenly` does not exist. `Often` is already an adverb.

hints: Manner adverbs go after the verb when there is no object.
After `drives`, `carefully` describes how she drives.

a27.carefully: Correct: `carefully` is a manner adverb after the verb `drives`.
a27.careful: Incorrect: `careful` is an adjective. Use the adverb `carefully`.
a27.carefully drives: Incorrect: the manner adverb goes after the verb.

hints: The place adverb `there` goes after the verb.
After `stayed`, `there` goes before the time adverbial `for two weeks`.

a28.there: Correct: `there` is a place adverb after the verb `stayed`.
a28.there stayed: Incorrect: place adverbs do not go before the verb in a declarative sentence.
a28.their: Incorrect: `their` is a possessive pronoun meaning 'belonging to them', not the place adverb `there`.

hints: `enough` goes after the adjective or adverb, not before it.
In `old ___ to drive`, `enough` goes after the adjective `old`.

a29.enough: Correct: `enough` goes after the adjective `old`: `old enough to drive`.
a29.enough old: Incorrect: `enough` goes after the adjective, not before it.
a29.enoughly: Incorrect: the form `enoughly` does not exist.

hints: With multiple adverbials, follow the order: Manner–Place–Time.
After `danced`, manner `gracefully` goes first, then place `at the party`, then time `last night`.

a30.gracefully: Correct: `gracefully` is a manner adverb placed first after the verb in MPT order.
a30.graceful: Incorrect: `graceful` is an adjective. Use the adverb `gracefully`.
a30.gracefully danced: Incorrect: the manner adverb goes after the verb.

hints: Comment adverbs (unfortunately, fortunately) go at the beginning of the sentence with a comma.
The gap is at the beginning before a comma — the typical position for a comment adverb.

a31.Unfortunately: Correct: `Unfortunately` is a comment adverb at the beginning of the sentence.
a31.Unfortunate: Incorrect: `unfortunate` is an adjective. The adverb `Unfortunately` is needed.
a31.Unfortunately we missed: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb, not extra words.

hints: In Perfect tenses, `just` goes between the auxiliary and the past participle.
In `has ___ finished`, `just` goes between `has` and `finished`.

a32.just: Correct: `just` goes between `has` and `finished` in Present Perfect.
a32.finished just: Incorrect: `just` does not go after the past participle in Present Perfect.
a32.just has: Incorrect: `just` goes after the auxiliary `has`, not before it.

hints: Manner adverbs go after the verb; `enough` goes after the adverb.
After `speaks`, `clearly` describes the manner, and `enough` follows it.

a33.clearly: Correct: `clearly` is a manner adverb after `speaks`, with `enough` following.
a33.clear: Incorrect: `clear` is an adjective. Use the adverb `clearly`.
a33.enough clearly: Incorrect: `enough` goes after the adverb, not before it.

hints: Time adverbs go at the end of the sentence.
After `will see you`, `soon` takes the final position.

a34.soon: Correct: `soon` is a time adverb at the end of the sentence.
a34.soon will: Incorrect: `soon` does not go before the modal `will` in this sentence.
a34.soonly: Incorrect: the form `soonly` does not exist. `Soon` is already an adverb.

hints: `How often` is used at the beginning to ask about frequency.
The question phrase `How often` goes before the auxiliary `do`.

a35.How often: Correct: `How often` is the question phrase for asking about frequency.
a35.Often how: Incorrect: the word order is fixed: `How often`, not the reverse.
a35.How much often: Incorrect: `How much often` has a redundant `much`. The correct form is `How often`.

hints: The frequency adverb `rarely` goes before the main verb.
Before `argue`, `rarely` indicates very low frequency.

a36.rarely: Correct: `rarely` goes before the main verb `argue`.
a36.argue rarely: Incorrect: in mid-position, frequency adverbs go before the main verb.
a36.rare: Incorrect: `rare` is an adjective. Use the adverb `rarely`.

hints: With multiple adverbials, follow Manner–Place–Time (MPT) order.
After `spoke`, manner `confidently` goes first, then place `at the conference`, then time `last week`.

a37.confidently: Correct: `confidently` goes first after the verb in the MPT chain.
a37.confident: Incorrect: `confident` is an adjective. Use the adverb `confidently`.
a37.confidently spoke: Incorrect: the manner adverb goes after the verb.

hints: In Present Perfect, adverbs go between the auxiliary and the past participle.
In `has ___ visited`, `already` goes between `has` and `visited`.

a38.already: Correct: `already` between `has` and `visited` in Present Perfect.
a38.visited already: Incorrect: `already` typically does not go after the past participle.
a38.already has: Incorrect: `already` goes after `has`, not before it in a positive sentence.

hints: Comment adverbs go at the beginning of the sentence with a comma.
The position before the comma at the beginning is typical for `Fortunately`.

a39.Fortunately: Correct: `Fortunately` is a comment adverb expressing a positive evaluation.
a39.Fortunate: Incorrect: `fortunate` is an adjective. Use the adverb `Fortunately`.
a39.Fortunately was: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb.

hints: Degree adverbs go between the auxiliary and the main verb.
In `would ___ like`, `really` goes between `would` and `like`.

a40.really: Correct: `really` between `would` and `like` intensifies the desire.
a40.real: Incorrect: `real` is an adjective. Use the adverb `really`.

hints: `How often` asks about frequency and goes at the beginning of the question.
Before `does`, the question phrase `How often` is needed.

a41.How often: Correct: `How often` asks about the frequency of an action.
a41.How many times often: Incorrect: `How many times` and `How often` are separate constructions; do not combine them.
a41.Often how: Incorrect: the word order is fixed: `How often`.

hints: Degree adverbs go before the adjective they modify.
In `were ___ excited`, `extremely` goes before `excited`.

a42.excited extremely: Incorrect: degree adverbs do not go after the adjective.
a42.extreme: Incorrect: `extreme` is an adjective. Use the adverb `extremely`.

hints: With multiple adverbials: Manner–Place–Time.
After `waited`, manner `patiently` goes first, then place `at the bus stop`, then time `for an hour`.

a43.patiently: Correct: `patiently` goes first after the verb in the MPT order.
a43.patient: Incorrect: `patient` is an adjective/noun. Use the adverb `patiently`.
a43.patiently waited: Incorrect: the manner adverb goes after the verb.

hints: The focusing adverb `almost` goes before the word it limits.
In the sentence, `almost` focuses on `only eats` — it goes before this phrase.

a44.almost: Correct: `almost` goes before `only eats`, limiting the scope.
a44.eats almost: Incorrect: `almost` does not go after the verb when focusing on `only`.

hints: An adverb can go between `to` and the verb (split infinitive).
In `to ___ check`, `carefully` goes between `to` and `check`.

a45.carefully: Correct: `carefully` in a split infinitive between `to` and `check`.
a45.careful: Incorrect: `careful` is an adjective. Use the adverb `carefully`.
a45.carefully to check: Incorrect: the adverb goes between `to` and the verb, not before `to`.

hints: `enough` goes after the adverb or adjective, not before.
In `fast ___ to win`, `enough` goes after `fast`.

a46.enough: Correct: `enough` after the adverb `fast`: `fast enough to win`.
a46.enough fast: Incorrect: `enough` goes after the adverb, not before it.
a46.enoughly: Incorrect: the form `enoughly` does not exist.

hints: With a chain of auxiliaries, the adverb can go after the last auxiliary.
In `have been ___ working`, `steadily` goes between `been` and `working`.

a47.steadily: Correct: `steadily` goes between `been` and `working` in Present Perfect Continuous.
a47.steady: Incorrect: `steady` is an adjective. Use the adverb `steadily`.
a47.steadily been: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before `been` in this construction.

hints: Viewpoint adverbs (Personally, Frankly) go at the beginning of the sentence with a comma.
The position before the comma at the beginning is for a viewpoint adverb.

a48.Personally: Correct: `Personally` is a viewpoint adverb at the beginning of the sentence.
a48.Personal: Incorrect: `personal` is an adjective. Use the adverb `Personally`.
a48.Personally I think: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb.

hints: The focusing adverb `only` goes before the element it restricts.
In `I ___ just wanted`, `only` goes before `just`, restricting the purpose.

a49.only: Correct: `only` before `just wanted` restricts the action.
a49.just only: Incorrect: `just only` is redundant; only `only` is needed.
a49.only just wanted: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb `only`.

hints: Manner adverbs go after the object.
After `read the instructions`, `thoroughly` describes the quality of reading.

a50.thoroughly: Correct: `thoroughly` is a manner adverb after the object `the instructions`.
a50.thorough: Incorrect: `thorough` is an adjective. Use the adverb `thoroughly`.
a50.thoroughly read: Incorrect: the manner adverb goes after the object.

hints: After `be`, adverbs go after the verb.
In `is ___ the best`, `probably` goes after `is`.

a51.probably: Correct: `probably` after `is` and before `the best student`.
a51.the probably best: Incorrect: `probably` does not go between the article and adjective.
a51.probable: Incorrect: `probable` is an adjective. Use the adverb `probably`.

hints: Adverbs go between the modal and the main verb.
In `should ___ leave`, `probably` goes between `should` and `leave`.

a52.probable: Incorrect: `probable` is an adjective. Use the adverb `probably`.

hints: `absolutely` goes before extreme adjectives like `amazing`.
The adjective `amazing` is extreme, so use `absolutely`, not `very`.

a53.amazing absolutely: Incorrect: degree adverbs do not go after the adjective.
a53.absolute: Incorrect: `absolute` is an adjective. Use the adverb `absolutely`.

hints: Adverbial order: Manner–Place–Time.
After `practised`, manner `diligently` goes first, then place `in the studio`, then time `all afternoon`.

a54.diligently: Correct: `diligently` — manner adverb first after the verb.
a54.diligent: Incorrect: `diligent` is an adjective. Use the adverb `diligently`.
a54.diligently practised: Incorrect: the manner adverb goes after the verb.

hints: Comment adverbs go at the beginning of the sentence expressing the speaker's evaluation.
The position before the comma at the beginning is for `Surprisingly`.

a55.Surprisingly: Correct: `Surprisingly` is a comment adverb at the beginning.
a55.Surprising: Incorrect: `surprising` is an adjective. Use the adverb `Surprisingly`.
a55.Surprisingly produced: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb.

hints: Adverbs go between the auxiliary and the main verb or next auxiliary.
In `would ___ have accepted`, `gladly` goes between `would` and `have`.

a56.glad: Incorrect: `glad` is an adjective. Use the adverb `gladly`.
a56.gladly would: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before the modal `would`.

hints: The focusing adverb `hardly` means 'barely' and goes before the verb.
In the sentence, `hardly` goes before `even noticed`, emphasising the minimal action.

a57.hardly: Correct: `hardly` before `even noticed` means 'barely even noticed'.
a57.hard: Incorrect: `hard` as an adverb means 'with great effort', not 'barely'. Use `hardly`.

hints: Split infinitive: an adverb goes between `to` and the verb.
In `to ___ reform`, `gradually` splits the infinitive.

a58.gradually: Correct: `gradually` in a split infinitive between `to` and `reform`.
a58.gradual: Incorrect: `gradual` is an adjective. Use the adverb `gradually`.
a58.gradually to reform: Incorrect: the adverb goes between `to` and the verb, not before `to`.

hints: Adverbial order after the verb: Manner–Place–Time.
After `performed`, manner `brilliantly` goes first, then place `on stage`, then time `last Saturday`.

a59.brilliantly: Correct: `brilliantly` goes first in the MPT chain.
a59.brilliant: Incorrect: `brilliant` is an adjective. Use the adverb `brilliantly`.
a59.brilliantly performed: Incorrect: the manner adverb goes after the verb.

hints: Degree adverbs go before the adjective.
In `were ___ different`, `significantly` goes before `different`.

a60.significant: Incorrect: `significant` is an adjective. Use the adverb `significantly`.
a60.significantly were: Incorrect: the degree adverb does not go before `were`.

hints: The focusing adverb `barely` goes between the modal and the main verb.
In `can ___ understand`, `barely` goes between `can` and `understand`.

a61.bare: Incorrect: `bare` is an adjective meaning 'naked'. Use the adverb `barely`.
a61.barely can: Incorrect: `barely` does not go before the modal `can` in a positive sentence.

hints: A time adverbial can be fronted for emphasis.
The position before the comma at the beginning is for the fronted time adverbial `Eventually`.

a62.Eventually: Correct: `Eventually` is a fronted time adverbial.
a62.Eventual: Incorrect: `eventual` is an adjective. Use the adverb `Eventually`.
a62.Eventually decided: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb.

hints: Frequency adverbs go before the main verb.
Before `only reads`, `generally` indicates a general tendency.

a63.generally: Correct: `generally` before `only reads` as a frequency adverb.
a63.general: Incorrect: `general` is an adjective. Use the adverb `generally`.

hints: Adverbial order: Manner–Place–Time.
After `discussed the matter`, manner `privately` goes first, then place, then time.

a64.privately: Correct: `privately` — manner adverb after the object, before place and time.
a64.private: Incorrect: `private` is an adjective. Use the adverb `privately`.
a64.privately discussed: Incorrect: the manner adverb goes after the verb/object.

hints: Adverbs go after the auxiliary chain before the main verb.
In `must have ___ forgotten`, `completely` goes after `have`.

a65.complete: Incorrect: `complete` is an adjective. Use the adverb `completely`.
a65.completely must: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before the modal `must`.

hints: A degree/scope adverb goes before the adjective.
In `is ___ well-known`, `internationally` defines the scope.

a66.international: Incorrect: `international` is an adjective. Use the adverb `internationally`.
a66.internationally is: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before `is` in this order.

hints: When a restrictive adverb (Only later, Only then) is fronted, inversion follows.
After `Only later`, inversion is needed: auxiliary before the subject.

a67.Only later: Correct: `Only later` at the beginning triggers inversion `did they realise`.
a67.Later only: Incorrect: the fixed order is `Only later`, not the reverse.
a67.Only later they: Incorrect: the gap needs only `Only later`; inversion `did they` already follows.

hints: `enough` goes after the adjective.
In `strong ___ to lift`, `enough` goes after `strong`.

a68.enough strong: Incorrect: `enough` goes after the adjective, not before it.
a68.enoughly: Incorrect: the form `enoughly` does not exist.

hints: `hardly` means 'almost not' and goes before `ever` in the phrase `hardly ever`.
In the sentence, `hardly ever` means 'almost never' — `hardly` goes before `ever`.

a69.hardly: Correct: `hardly ever` is a fixed phrase where `hardly` precedes `ever`.
a69.ever hardly: Incorrect: the fixed order is `hardly ever`, not the reverse.

hints: Degree adverbs go after `be` and before the adjective.
In `were ___ aware`, `fully` goes before `aware`.

a70.full: Incorrect: `full` is an adjective. Use the adverb `fully`.
a70.fully were: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before `were` in a positive sentence.

hints: Split infinitive: an adverb goes between `to` and the verb.
In `to ___ consider`, `thoroughly` splits the infinitive.

a71.thoroughly: Correct: `thoroughly` in a split infinitive between `to` and `consider`.
a71.thorough: Incorrect: `thorough` is an adjective. Use the adverb `thoroughly`.
a71.thoroughly to: Incorrect: the adverb goes between `to` and the verb, not before `to`.

hints: Comment adverbs go at the beginning with a comma.
The position at the beginning with a comma is for `Remarkably`.

a72.Remarkably: Correct: `Remarkably` is a comment adverb expressing surprise.
a72.Remarkable: Incorrect: `remarkable` is an adjective. Use the adverb `Remarkably`.
a72.Remarkably managed: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb.

hints: After the negative adverb `Never` at the beginning, subject-auxiliary inversion is required.
After `Never`, the correct order is: `have I seen` (auxiliary + subject + past participle).

a73.I have seen: Incorrect: `Never` at the beginning requires inversion, not normal word order.
a73.I seen have: Incorrect: both the inversion and Present Perfect structure are wrong.

hints: After `Rarely` at the beginning, inversion is required.
The correct order: auxiliary `does` + subject `the media` + main verb `pay`.

a74.does the media pay: Correct: inversion after `Rarely` — `does the media pay`.
a74.the media pays: Incorrect: `Rarely` at the beginning requires inversion.
a74.the media does pay: Incorrect: in inversion, the subject goes between the auxiliary and main verb.

hints: In a passive with a modal, the adverb can go after the last auxiliary before the main verb.
In `should be ___ interpreted`, `always` goes after `be`.

a75.always: Correct: `always` between `be` and `interpreted` in the passive construction.
a75.interpreted always: Incorrect: the adverb does not go after the past participle in the passive.
a75.always should: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before the modal `should`.

hints: Viewpoint adverbs go at the beginning with a comma.
`Arguably` means 'it can be argued' and goes at the beginning.

a76.Arguably: Correct: `Arguably` is a viewpoint adverb at the beginning.
a76.Arguable: Incorrect: `arguable` is an adjective. Use the adverb `Arguably`.
a76.Arguably has: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb.

hints: After `Seldom` at the beginning, inversion is required.
The correct order: `is there` — inversion in the existential construction.

a77.there is: Incorrect: `Seldom` at the beginning requires inversion.
a77.there are: Incorrect: no inversion, and `vote` is singular, so `are` does not agree.

hints: Focusing adverbs go before the element they focus on.
`specifically` before `highlights` focuses attention on the particular action.

a78.specifically: Correct: `specifically` before `highlights` as a focusing adverb.
a78.specific: Incorrect: `specific` is an adjective. Use the adverb `specifically`.
a78.specifically highlights the: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb.

hints: Adverbs go after the auxiliary chain before the main verb.
In `could have ___ avoided`, `easily` goes after `have`.

a79.easily: Correct: `easily` between `have` and `avoided` in the modal perfect.
a79.easy: Incorrect: `easy` is an adjective. Use the adverb `easily`.
a79.easily could: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before the modal `could`.

hints: The correlative `Not only … but also` structure: after inversion in the first clause, the second starts with `but also`.
After `Not only did she …`, the second clause starts with `but also`.

a80.but also: Correct: `but also` is the second part of `Not only … but also`.
a80.and also: Incorrect: the correlative requires `but also`, not `and also`.
a80.but as well: Incorrect: the standard pair is `Not only … but also`.

hints: In the passive, adverbial order: Manner–Place–Time.
After `were handled`, manner `discreetly` goes first, then place and time.

a81.discreetly: Correct: `discreetly` — manner adverb after `handled`, before place and time.
a81.discreet: Incorrect: `discreet` is an adjective. Use the adverb `discreetly`.
a81.discreetly handled: Incorrect: the manner adverb goes after the verb.

hints: After `Little` at the beginning, inversion is required.
The correct order: `did they know` — auxiliary + subject + main verb.

a82.they knew: Incorrect: `Little` at the beginning requires inversion.
a82.they did know: Incorrect: the subject goes between the auxiliary and main verb.

hints: Split infinitive: an adverb between `to` and the verb.
In `to ___ increase`, `significantly` splits the infinitive.

a83.significantly: Correct: `significantly` in a split infinitive between `to` and `increase`.
a83.significant: Incorrect: `significant` is an adjective. Use the adverb `significantly`.
a83.significantly to: Incorrect: the adverb goes between `to` and the verb.

hints: Comment adverbs go at the beginning with a comma.
The position before the comma at the beginning is for `Interestingly`.

a84.Interestingly: Correct: `Interestingly` is a comment adverb at the beginning.
a84.Interesting: Incorrect: `interesting` is an adjective. Use the adverb `Interestingly`.
a84.Interestingly suggests: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb.

hints: In the passive, manner adverbs go between the auxiliary and the past participle.
In `was ___ rejected`, `unanimously` goes between `was` and `rejected`.

a85.unanimously: Correct: `unanimously` between `was` and `rejected` in the passive.
a85.unanimous: Incorrect: `unanimous` is an adjective. Use the adverb `unanimously`.
a85.unanimously was: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before `was`.

hints: Manner adverbs go after the verb; `enough` goes after the adverb.
After `speaks`, `fluently` describes the manner, with `enough` following.

a86.fluently: Correct: `fluently` is a manner adverb after `speaks`, followed by `enough`.
a86.fluent: Incorrect: `fluent` is an adjective. Use the adverb `fluently`.
a86.enough fluently: Incorrect: `enough` goes after the adverb, not before it.

hints: After `Only after …`, inversion is required: auxiliary before the subject.
After `Only after the audit`, the auxiliary `did` is needed for Past Simple inversion.

a87.did: Correct: `did` is the auxiliary for Past Simple inversion after `Only after …`.
a87.were: Incorrect: `become` requires `did` for Past Simple, not `were`.
a87.had: Incorrect: `had` is for Past Perfect, but the sentence is in Past Simple.

hints: `scarcely` means 'barely' and goes before `ever`.
The phrase `scarcely ever` means 'almost never' — `scarcely` precedes `ever`.

a88.scarce: Incorrect: `scarce` is an adjective. Use the adverb `scarcely`.
a88.ever scarcely: Incorrect: the fixed order is `scarcely ever`.

hints: Manner adverbs can go before the main verb for emphasis.
In `The study ___ suggests`, `convincingly` before `suggests` adds emphasis.

a89.convincingly: Correct: `convincingly` before `suggests` emphasises the strength of the suggestion.
a89.convincing: Incorrect: `convincing` is an adjective. Use the adverb `convincingly`.
a89.convincingly study: Incorrect: the gap needs only the adverb.

hints: Adverbs go between the modal and the main verb.
In `must ___ reconsider`, `urgently` goes between `must` and `reconsider`.

a90.urgent: Incorrect: `urgent` is an adjective. Use the adverb `urgently`.
a90.urgently must: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before the modal `must`.

hints: `Not until …` at the beginning requires inversion in the main clause.
After `Not until the final chapter`, the auxiliary `does` is needed for Present Simple inversion.

a91.does: Correct: `does` for Present Simple inversion after `Not until …`.
a91.did: Incorrect: the sentence is in Present Simple (`reveal`), so `does` is needed.
a91.the author does: Incorrect: the gap needs only `does`; the subject already follows.

hints: After `Nowhere else`, inversion is required: auxiliary + subject + main verb.
The correct order: `will you find`.

a92.will you find: Correct: inversion after `Nowhere else` — `will you find`.
a92.you find will: Incorrect: the word order is completely wrong.

hints: In the passive, a comment adverb can go between the auxiliary and the main verb.
In `was ___ meticulously proofread`, `reportedly` goes after `was`.

a93.reportedly: Correct: `reportedly` is a comment adverb between `was` and `meticulously proofread`.
a93.reported: Incorrect: `reported` is a past participle, not a comment adverb.
a93.reportedly was: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before `was` in a positive passive.

hints: After `Under no circumstances`, inversion is required: modal + subject + verb.
The correct order: `should you share`.

a94.you should share: Incorrect: a negative adverbial at the beginning requires inversion.
a94.you share should: Incorrect: the sentence structure is completely wrong.

hints: `So + adverb + inversion` expresses consequence/result.
After `So`, with an adverb, inversion is needed: `brilliantly did she perform`.

a95.brilliantly did she perform: Correct: `So brilliantly did she perform` — inversion for emphasis.

hints: A comparative adverb can go in a split infinitive.
In `to ___ redistribute`, the comparative adverb `more equitably` is needed.

a96.more equitably: Correct: `more equitably` is a comparative adverb in a split infinitive.
a96.equitable more: Incorrect: in the comparative, `more` goes before the adverb.
a96.more equitable: Incorrect: `equitable` is an adjective. Use the adverb `equitably`.

hints: After `Only by …`, inversion is required: modal + subject.
The correct order: `can we`.

a97.can we: Correct: inversion after `Only by …` — `can we arrive`.
a97.we are able to: Incorrect: inversion requires a modal verb, not `be able to`.

hints: `Hardly … when` requires inversion with Past Perfect in the first clause.
After `Hardly`, the correct order: `had we sat down` (Past Perfect inversion).

a98.had we sat down: Correct: `Hardly had we sat down when …` — Past Perfect inversion.
a98.we sat down: Incorrect: `Hardly … when` requires Past Perfect, not Past Simple.

hints: `scarcely` goes between the modal and the next auxiliary.
In `can ___ be attributed`, `scarcely` goes between `can` and `be`.

a99.scarce: Incorrect: `scarce` is an adjective. Use the adverb `scarcely`.
a99.scarcely can: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before the modal `can`.

hints: `Needless to say` is a fixed comment phrase at the beginning with a comma.
The expression `Needless to say` means 'it goes without saying'.

a100.Needless to say: Correct: `Needless to say` is a fixed comment phrase at the beginning.
a100.Needlessly to say: Incorrect: the fixed expression is `Needless to say`, not `Needlessly`.
a100.Needless saying: Incorrect: the correct form is `Needless to say`.

hints: `No sooner … than` requires inversion with Past Perfect.
After `No sooner`, the correct order: `had she arrived` (Past Perfect inversion).

a101.had she arrived: Correct: `No sooner had she arrived than …` — Past Perfect inversion.
a101.she arrived: Incorrect: `No sooner … than` requires Past Perfect, not Past Simple.

hints: The auxiliary goes before the manner adverb in compound tenses.
Before `painstakingly recreated`, the auxiliary `had` is needed for Past Perfect.

a102.had: Correct: `had` — Past Perfect auxiliary before the manner adverb `painstakingly`.
a102.has: Incorrect: the context (over several months — past) requires Past Perfect `had`, not `has`.
a102.was: Incorrect: `was` forms Past Continuous/Passive, but `recreated` needs `had`.

hints: After `In no way`, inversion is required: auxiliary + subject + verb.
The correct order: `does this diminish`.

a103.does this diminish: Correct: inversion after `In no way` — `does this diminish`.
a103.this diminishes: Incorrect: the negative adverbial requires inversion.
a103.this does diminish: Incorrect: the subject goes between auxiliary and main verb in inversion.

hints: In the passive with an auxiliary chain, adverbs go after the last auxiliary.
In `has been ___ praised`, `widely` goes after `been`.

a104.widely: Correct: `widely` after `been` and before `praised` in Present Perfect Passive.
a104.wide: Incorrect: for abstract meaning, use `widely`, not `wide`.
a104.widely been: Incorrect: the adverb goes after `been`, not before it.

hints: After `At no point`, inversion is required: verb/auxiliary + subject.
The correct order: `was it` — inversion of the verb `be`.

a105.was it: Correct: inversion after `At no point` — `was it my intention`.
a105.it was: Incorrect: the negative adverbial requires inversion.
a105.it had been: Incorrect: no inversion, and the tense does not match.

hints: Degree adverbs go after the auxiliary chain before the past participle.
In `has been ___ challenged`, `increasingly` goes after `been`.

a106.increasing: Incorrect: `increasing` is an adjective. Use the adverb `increasingly`.
a106.increasingly has: Incorrect: the adverb does not go before `has`.

hints: After `Only when …`, inversion is required in the main clause.
The correct order: `can researchers` — modal + subject.

a107.can researchers: Correct: inversion after `Only when …` — `can researchers draw`.
a107.researchers are able to: Incorrect: inversion requires a modal, not `be able to`.

hints: After `Not for a moment`, inversion is required: auxiliary + subject + verb.
The correct order: `did we suspect` — Past Simple inversion.

a108.did we suspect: Correct: inversion after `Not for a moment` — `did we suspect`.
a108.we suspected: Incorrect: the negative adverbial requires inversion.
a108.we did suspect: Incorrect: the subject goes between auxiliary and main verb.
